front.title: The Technological Singularity
front.subtitle: A hypothetical point in time where autonomous AI growth becomes uncontrollable and irreversible.

back.text:
The Singularity is a theoretical future event triggered by the development of artificial general intelligence (AGI). According to this hypothesis, an upgradable intelligent agent would enter a 'runaway self-improvement cycle,' where each new version of the AI designs a more capable successor at an exponential rate. This process would result in an intelligence explosion, far surpassing all human cognitive abilities and fundamentally altering civilization.

While the concept is a staple of science fiction, it is rooted in computer science and mathematics. Mathematician John von Neumann first discussed the idea in the 1950s, and it was later popularized by author Vernor Vinge and futurist Ray Kurzweil. They argue that the accelerating pace of technological progress follows Moore's Law and other exponential trends, eventually reaching a point where human prediction becomes impossible.

Critics and researchers debate the feasibility of the Singularity, citing physical limits of hardware, the complexity of consciousness, and the 'diminishing returns' of software optimization. Whether a looming reality or a philosophical metaphor, the Singularity serves as a critical framework for discussing AI safety, ethics, and the long-term trajectory of human-machine evolution.
